paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — Admiralty Secondary School Preliminary 2021 SA2 Paper 2 §B

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about Jamie's journey to a diamond mine and answer the questions that follow. Section B [20 marks] from Admiralty Secondary School Preliminary Examination 2021 Paper 1128/02. (Section A on the Disney+ advertisement and Section C on healthy relationships are omitted from this auto-graded set — the original Section B carries 12 questions worth 20 marks; this fixture ingests the 9 short-answer items that have a single objective canonical answer plus 3 MCQ items derived from the original Q16 gist task.)
sections.passageTitle: Jamie's journey to Klipdrift (Master of the Game by Sidney Sheldon)
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The three men, with Jamie in the middle, squeezed into the seat, crowded against each other, their knees cramped, their backs pressing against the wooden back of the driver's seat. There was no room to move or breathe, but Jamie didn't care. 'Hold on!' the driver sang out, and a moment later, they were racing through the sands of Cape Town on their way to the diamond fields at Klipdrift.

Paragraph 2
By bullock wagon, the journey was relatively comfortable. They were large and roomy, with tent covers to ward off the blazing winter sun. Each wagon accommodated a dozen passengers and was drawn by teams of horses. Refreshments were provided at regular stations. The cart he was in was different.

Paragraph 3
It never stopped, except to change drivers and horses. The pace was a full gallop, over rough roads and fields and rutted trails. There were no springs on the cart and each bounce was like the blow of a horse's hoof to one's chest. Jamie gritted his teeth and thought — I can stand it until we stop for the night. I'll eat and get some sleep and in the morning I will be fine. But when night time came, there was a halt just for a change of driver and horse and they were off again.

Paragraph 4
'When do we stop to eat?' Jamie asked. Someone laughed. 'We don't,' the driver grunted. Jamie stared at him.

Paragraph 5
They rode through the long night, travelling over dusty, bumpy roads by moonlight. The little cart bouncing up the rises, plunging down the valleys. Every nerve of Jamie's body shrieked. He was exhausted, but it was impossible to sleep. Every time he started to doze, he was jarred awake. His body was cramped and aching and there was no room to stretch. It was a six-hundred-mile journey and he was not sure if he was going to live through it. Neither was he sure that he wanted to.

Paragraph 6
When the next dawn came, the real wilderness began. Stretching to infinity, the monotonous desert lay flat and intimidating under the pitiless sun. The passengers were smothered in heat, dust and flies.

Paragraph 7
'I'm going to make it. I'm going to make it.' The words drummed in his head with the rhythm of the wheels.

Paragraph 8
True enough, he saw that first glint in the sands.

(Adapted from Master of the Game by Sidney Sheldon)

questions.stem: Q5. Paragraph 1 mentioned that 'There was no room to move or breathe'. Identify a sentence in the same paragraph that suggests the same idea. [1]
questions.answer: three men, with Jamie in the middle, squeezed into the seat, crowded against each other

questions.stem: Q7(a). What does the word 'racing' (Paragraph 1) suggest about the cart? [1]
Q7(b). Identify a phrase from Paragraph 3 that conveys the same meaning as 'racing'. [1]
questions.answer: the pace was a full gallop

questions.stem: Q8. What does the phrase 'like the blow of a horse's hoof to one's chest' suggest about 'each bounce'? [1]
Q9. What does the word 'halt' (Paragraph 3) suggest about the interval? [1]
Q10. From Paragraph 4, why did Jamie stare at the driver? [1]
questions.answer: he was shocked that they were not going to eat

questions.stem: Q12. From Paragraph 5, 'It was a six-hundred-mile journey and he was not sure if he was going to live through it. Neither was he sure that he wanted to.' How did Jamie feel? [1]
questions.answer: desperate for the journey to end

questions.stem: Q14. From Paragraph 7, how does the writer show Jamie's determination? [1]
Q15. From Paragraph 8, what was the 'glint'? [1]

sections.instruction: Q16. The text reflects the author's emotions. For each paragraph below, choose the emotion that best summarises the dominant feeling. The original task (Q16, [3]) asks students to pick 3 emotions from a 6-word bank: Optimistic / Dread / Melancholic / Excited / Shocked / Miserable. We split it into three single-mark MCQ items here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same Jamie's journey narrative in Exercise 1 above. The dominant emotion in each part of the text shifts as Jamie's experience changes — from anticipation at the start, to disbelief mid-journey, to despair in the desert. Pick the single best emotion for each section.

questions.stem: Q16(i). What is the dominant emotion in Paragraph 1 (Jamie squeezed into the cart, on his way to the diamond fields)? [1]
Q16(ii). What is the dominant emotion in Paragraphs 3-4 (the cart never stopped; Jamie asked when they would eat)? [1]
Q16(iii). What is the dominant emotion in Paragraphs 5-6 (riding through the long night; the desert under the pitiless sun)? [1]
